full_notes:
Work Energy and Power is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cHAPTER SIX SYSTEMS OF PARTICLES AND ROTATIONAL MOTION 6.1 INTRODUCTION In the earlier chapters we primarily considered the motion of a single particle.

The text explains that (A particle is ideally represented as a point mass having no size.) We applied the results of our study even to the motion of bodies of finite size, assuming that motion of such bodies can be described in terms of the motion of a particle.

The text explains that any real body which we encounter in daily life has a finite size.

The text explains that in dealing with the motion of extended bodies (bodies of finite size) often the idealised model of a particle is inadequate.

The text explains that in this chapter we shall try to go beyond this inadequacy.

The text explains that we shall attempt to build an understanding of the motion of extended bodies.

The text explains that an extended body, in the first place, is a system of particles.

The text explains that we shall begin with the consideration of motion of the system as a whole.

The text explains that the centre of mass of a system of particles will be a key concept here.

The text explains that we shall discuss the motion of the centre of mass of a system of particles and usefulness of this concept in understanding the motion of extended bodies.

The text explains that a large class of problems with extended bodies can be solved by considering them to be rigid bodies.

The text explains that ideally a rigid body is a body with a perfectly definite and unchanging shape.

The text explains that the distances between all pairs of particles of such a body do not change.

The text explains that it is evident from this definition of a rigid body that no real body is truly rigid, since real bodies deform under the influence of forces.

The text explains that but in many situations the deformations are negligible.

The text explains that in a number of situations involving bodies such as wheels, tops, steel beams, molecules and planets on the other hand, we can ignore that they warp (twist out of shape), bend or vibrate and treat them as rigid.

The text explains that let us try to explore this question by taking some examples of the motion of rigid bodies.

The text explains that let us begin with a rectangular 6.1 Introduction 6.2 Centre of mass 6.3 Motion of centre of mass 6.4 Linear momentum of a system of particles 6.5 Vector product of two vectors 6.6 Angular velocity and its relation with linear velocity 6.7 Torque and angular momentum 6.8 Equilibrium of a rigid body 6.9 Moment of inertia 6.10 Kinematics of rotational motion about a fixed axis 6.11 Dynamics of rotational motion about a fixed axis 6.12 Angular momentum in case of rotation about a fixed axis Summary Points to Ponder Exercises

The text explains that sYSTEMS OF PARTICLES AND ROTATIONAL MOTION 9 3 block sliding down an inclined plane without any sidewise movement.

The text explains that sYSTEMS OF PARTICLES AND ROTATIONAL MOTION 9 5 axis of rotation of such a fan has an oscillating (sidewise) movement in a horizontal plane about the vertical through the point at which the axis is pivoted (point O in Fig.

The text explains that we shall, in the present chapter, consider rotational motion about a fixed axis only.

The text explains that 6.2 CENTRE OF MASS We shall first see what the centre of mass of a system of particles is and then discuss its significance.

The text explains that for simplicity we shall start with a two particle system.

The text explains that notice, in this case the orientation of OP, i.e. the angle OP makes with a fixed direction, say the horizontal, remains the same, i.e. α1 = α2 = α3.

The text explains that the centre of mass of the system is that point C which is at a distance X from O, where X is given by 1 1 2 2 1 2 m x m x X m m + = + (6.1) In Eq.

The text explains that if we have n particles of masses m1, m2, ...mn respectively, along a straight line taken as the x- axis, then by definition the position of the centre of the mass of the system of particles is given by.

The text explains that the sum i m M = ∑ is the total mass of the system.

The text explains that the centre of mass C of the system of the three particles is defined and located by the coordinates (X, Y) given by 1 1 2 2 3 3 1 2 3 m x m x m x X m m m + + = + + (6.3a) 1 1 2 2 3 3 1 2 3 m y m y m y Y m m m + + = + + (6.3b) For the particles of equal mass m = m1 = m2 = m3, 1 2 3 1 2 3 ( ) 3 3 m x x x x x x X m + + + + = = 1 2 3 1 2 3 ( ) 3 3 m y y y y y y Y m + + + + = = Thus, for three particles of equal mass, the centre of mass coincides with the centroid of the triangle formed by the particles.

The text explains that (6.3a) and (6.3b) are generalised easily to a system of n particles, not necessarily lying in a plane, but distributed in space.

The text explains that the centre of mass of such a system is at (X, Y, Z ), where i i m x X M = ∑ (6.4a) i i m y Y M = ∑ (6.4b) and i i m z Z M = ∑ (6.4c) Here M = i m ∑ is the total mass of the system.

The text explains that let ir be the position vector of the ith particle and R be the position vector of the centre of mass:  i i i i x y z = + + r i j k ɵ ɵ and  X Y Z = + + R i j k ɵ ɵ Then i i m M = ∑ r R (6.4d) The sum on the right hand side is a vector sum.

The text explains that note the economy of expressions we achieve by use of vectors.

The text explains that if the origin of the frame of reference (the coordinate system) is chosen to be the centre of mass then 0 i i m = ∑ r for the given system of particles.

The text explains that a rigid body, such as a metre stick or a flywheel, is a system of closely packed particles; Eqs.

The text explains that sYSTEMS OF PARTICLES AND ROTATIONAL MOTION 9 7 small, we can treat the body as a continuous distribution of mass.

The text explains that the coordinates of the centre of mass now are X M x m Y M y m Z M z m = = = ∫ ∫ ∫ 1 1 1 d d d , and (6.5a) The vector expression equivalent to these three scalar expressions is R r = ∫ 1 M m d (6.5b) If we choose, the centre of mass as the origin of our coordinate system, = R 0 i.e., r 0 dm = ∫ or x m y m z m d d d = = = ∫ ∫ ∫ 0 (6.6) Often we have to calculate the centre of mass of homogeneous bodies of regular shapes like rings, discs, spheres, rods etc.

The text explains that sYSTEMS OF PARTICLES AND ROTATIONAL MOTION 9 9 of Fig.

The text explains that how will you then determine the centre of mass of the lamina? ⊳ 6.3 MOTION OF CENTRE OF MASS Equipped with the definition of the centre of mass, we are now in a position to discuss its physical importance for a system of n particles.

The text explains that differentiating Eq.(6.8) with respect to time, we obtain 1 2 1 2 d d d d ... d d d d n n M m m m t t t t = + + + v v v V or 1 1 2 2 ... n n M m m m = + + + A a a a (6.9) where ( ) 1 1 d /dt = a v is the acceleration of the first particle, ( ) 2 2 d /dt = a v is the acceleration of the second particle etc. and ( ) d / dt = A V is the acceleration of the centre of mass of the system of particles.

The text explains that (6.9) may be written as 1 2 ... n M = + + + A F F F (6.10) Thus, the total mass of a system of particles times the acceleration of its centre of mass is the vector sum of all the forces acting on the system of particles.

The text explains that among these forces on each particle there will be external forces exerted by bodies outside the system and also internal forces exerted by the particles on one another.

The text explains that (6.10) as ext M = A F (6.11) where ext F represents the sum of all external forces acting on the particles of the system.

The text explains that (6.11) states that the centre of mass of a system of particles moves as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were applied at that point.

The text explains that notice, to determine the motion of the centre of mass no knowledge of internal forces of the system of particles is required; for this purpose we need to know only the external forces.

The text explains that (6.11) we did not need to specify the nature of the system of particles.

The text explains that the system may be a collection of particles in which there may be all kinds of internal motions, or it may be a rigid body which has either pure translational motion or a combination of translational and rotational motion.

The text explains that (Note: uniform angular velocity in the absence of friction implies zero torque.

The text explains that in practice, applied torque is needed to counter frictional torque).

The text explains that 6.15 From a uniform disk of radius R, a circular hole of radius R/2 is cut out.

The text explains that the centre of the hole is at R/2 from the centre of the original disc.

The text explains that locate the centre of gravity of the resulting flat body.

The text explains that 6.16 A metre stick is balanced on a knife edge at its centre.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Physics, Chapter 6: Work Energy and Power
